body_txt: Improv, short for Improvisation, is the act of going off-roading from the set script and making up entire chunks of dialogue or characterisation. This is similar to an ad-lib (a short — usually one or two lines — deviation), but here the connotations are that it happens frequently, if not actually the entire acting method. This varies wildly from individual lines to half of the script. Many examples of Throw It In are because of the actor goofing around between takes or the actor stays in character as a mistake happens. It can also be used to produce Enforced Method Acting , one actor is told to go off script to get a realistic reaction from another actor. Other times the dialogue in the script is untouched but they discover Ambiguous Syntax or Accent On The Wrong Syllable that turns it into a joke. Theatresports is this applied to live performances, organized improvisational theatre. It's largely based around specific improv exercises, telling entire stories off the cuff and can include players competing with each other over how long they can keep up a gag before the other breaks . Whose Line Is It Anyway? is the most famous theatresport as produced for television. Two famous Improv troupes, Second City from Chicago and The Groundlings from Los Angeles, have been a recruiting ground for shows like Saturday Night Live . Note that this trope is not always a good thing. If done poorly and without moderation, improvisation may lead to meandering and repetitive performances and conversations that could drag out scenes for far longer than they should. If the actors are not cast for their skill in improv it can harm the performance, either watching them desperately try to come up with something interesting or result in a bombastic performance that may not be appropriate for the scene as it was conceived. Compare Realistic Diction Is Unrealistic , as carefully scripted and precisely performed monologues don't reflect natural inflection and Verbal Tics . If a work shifts from one style to another, Improv may be at play. One way to see where a show or movie tends to use this is when there are Hilarious Outtakes and a certain line changes depending on the take that was used. Compare Harpo Does Something Funny where the script has a gap left with only the instruction "[actor] does something funny here." (done when an actor is known to be good at improv, usually coming up with something funnier than the writers can, so the script deliberately gives an opening for them to improv for a while before getting back on track).
